Landing.availableBanner: Available for Contract & Full-time Roles · AI/ML engineering · RAG systems · retrieval architecture · UK-based, remote-friendly
Landing.heroContext: Real enterprise knowledge looks like this: incident reports, supplier updates, customer feedback, staffing memos — scattered across systems, written by different people, never designed to be queried together.
Landing.title: Adaptive RAG Playground
Landing.subtitle: Compare semantic search and graph traversal on the same query, then see the auto-router explain why it chose each method.

features.graphDesc: 201 entities, 175 relationships, 8 documents — follows the chain.
features.compareDesc: Same query, two methods, side by side. See which one wins.
features.decisionDesc: The router explains its choice — signals, entity count, confidence.

comparePreview.label: COMPARE MODE
comparePreview.diffNote: Graph found file 04 (Google Review complaint) and file 08 (mobile order) via entity chain — semantic missed them because "Google Review" doesn't appear in file 03.
comparePreview.caption: Same query, two methods — graph reconstructs what semantic misses.

dataset.heading: BrewPulse Coffee
dataset.p1: Real enterprise knowledge looks like this: incident reports, supplier updates, customer feedback, staffing memos — scattered across systems, written by different people, never designed to be queried together.
dataset.p2Before: BrewPulse Coffee simulates exactly that. The only way to answer
dataset.p2After: from these eight documents is to read all of them manually — or use RAG.
dataset.p3: Synthetic corpus, manually curated. Full methodology and engineering decisions on the dataset page.
dataset.chainLabel: THE CENTRAL CAUSAL CHAIN — Q20 DEMO HIGHLIGHT
dataset.chainNote: No single document tells this story. Graph traversal reconstructs it; semantic search misses hops 2 and 3.

Landing.certBuiltWith: This project built with Claude Code agentic workflow · CLAUDE.md context preservation · custom extractor via Claude tool use
Landing.footer: Built by Mustafa Barış Akverdi · Senior Oracle APEX/DBA + AI developer · Reading, UK

Dataset.title: BrewPulse
Dataset.subtitle: Synthetic operational corpus for Retrieval-Augmented Generation demos. Eight interconnected documents simulating a real enterprise knowledge base — where no single file answers any meaningful question alone.
Dataset.methodologyNote: AI-generated, manually curated. Designed with deliberate retrieval failure modes: indirect references, multi-hop causal chains, and ambiguous attribution.
Dataset.causalDesc: This four-hop chain spans four files — the kind of reasoning that requires multi-document retrieval to reconstruct. No single document tells this full story. This is Query 20 in the demo: the wow highlight.
Dataset.causalChainLabel: Wakefield depot failure → Google Review complaint
Dataset.causalWhyBody: Graph traversal reconstructs the full chain by following entity links (NorthBrew → Wakefield → oat milk shortage → mobile order → complaint). Semantic search retrieves isolated fragments — it finds the complaint and the depot failure, but cannot reliably bridge all four hops.
Dataset.entityDesc: Named entities act as retrieval anchors. The more files an entity appears in, the more it can bridge unrelated-seeming documents during graph traversal.
Dataset.queriesDesc: All 20 questions require retrieval across multiple documents. None can be answered correctly from a single file. Grouped by theme for demo navigation.
Dataset.challengesDesc: Six deliberate design properties ensure the dataset rewards multi-document retrieval and punishes naive keyword search or single-document lookup.

indirect.title: Indirect References
indirect.desc: Causes and effects are named differently across documents. The system must bridge vocabulary gaps semantically.
indirect.example: "Watery espresso" complaints in file 04 ← valve pressure failure in file 02 — never explicitly linked.

multiHop.title: Multi-Hop Causal Chains
multiHop.desc: The full cause-effect story spans 3–4 documents. Single-document retrieval gives an incomplete and potentially misleading answer.
multiHop.example: Wakefield depot → oat milk shortage → mobile order fail → Google Review. Files 03→01→08→04.

attribution.title: Ambiguous Attribution
attribution.desc: Some complaints could plausibly be blamed on the app, the supplier, or the equipment. Only multi-doc retrieval disambiguates.
attribution.example: Was the Manchester oat milk complaint about the POS bug or the NorthBrew shortfall? Answer: both, in different weeks.

timeline.title: Timeline References
timeline.desc: February events appear in March documents as assumed context. The system must reconstruct timelines across report dates.
timeline.example: The fault "first noticed mid-February" in file 02 is the same event described as a "six-week issue" in file 01.

partial.title: Partial Information
partial.desc: Each document holds a piece of the puzzle. A correct answer requires synthesising fragments from 2–5 files.
partial.example: Leeds fault: described technically in 02, operationally in 01, impact in 04, fleet risk in 06, KPI outcome in 07.

control.title: Control Case (Sheffield)
control.desc: Sheffield's good performance is explained by a decision documented across two separate files — requiring synthesis to understand why.
control.example: Rosa Chen's buffer stock decision (file 07) traces back to the Sep 2023 NorthBrew disruption (file 03).

Dataset.methodologyDesc: BrewPulse Coffee is synthetic — generated with Claude and manually curated. This is disclosed openly because the methodology itself demonstrates the engineering skill, not a limitation to hide.

gen.heading: AI-assisted synthetic corpus design
gen.body: Documents were generated with Claude, co-designed with deliberate retrieval failure modes in mind. Entity recurrence, vocabulary gaps, and multi-hop dependencies were specified upfront — not left to chance.

cur.heading: Manual curation and ground truth
cur.body: Each of the 20 queries has a manually verified ground truth mapping: expected files, expected retrieval method winner, and the reasoning. This enables precision/recall evaluation of any retrieval change.

ext.heading: Custom graph extractor (not off-shelf)
ext.body: Graphify v0.8.13 was tested and rejected — 30–40% entity recall and a streaming bug. A custom extractor was written using Claude tool use with domain-specific entity typology and confidence scoring. Build cost: $0.11.

emb.heading: File-level chunking via Voyage AI
emb.body: Documents are short (~640 words). File-level chunking is used — no sliding window needed. Voyage AI voyage-3 embeddings stored in pgvector on Supabase. Total embedding cost: $0.0004.

Dataset.architectureDesc: End-to-end flow from query to streamed response — showing how the heuristic router, retrieval paths, and LLM fit together.
Dataset.architectureNote: Build-time: BrewPulse corpus → custom extractor (Claude tool use) → graph.json. Voyage AI embeddings → pgvector. Query-time: heuristic router classifies, paths execute in parallel for Compare mode, results fed to Claude Haiku for streaming response. Decision log emits the router's reasoning.
Dataset.ctaDesc: Try the 20 queries live — see graph vs semantic side by side.
Dataset.footer: Built by Mustafa Barış Akverdi · Senior Oracle APEX/DBA + AI developer · Reading, UK

Playground.title: Adaptive RAG Playground
Playground.placeholder: Ask about BrewPulse Coffee North England ops… (Ctrl+Enter)
Playground.emptyTitle: Select a query from the sidebar or type your own.
Playground.emptyHint: Start with Q20 — then switch to Compare to see graph vs semantic side by side.
Playground.graphOnlyNote: These extra hops complete the causal chain — semantic search didn't reach them.
Playground.footer: Built by Mustafa Barış Akverdi · Senior Oracle APEX/DBA + AI developer · Reading, UK

prompts.1: What impact did NorthBrew Supplies have across branches in Q1 2024?
prompts.2: Which branches were affected by the oat milk shortage and what were the consequences?
prompts.3: Had NorthBrew Supplies caused problems before the Q1 2024 incident?
prompts.4: What steps were recommended to reduce dependency on NorthBrew Supplies?
prompts.5: Which branches experienced espresso machine failures and what was the common cause?
prompts.6: What is the relationship between the equipment fault at Leeds and the one at Manchester?
prompts.7: Is there a risk that espresso machine failures could recur at other branches?
prompts.8: Which branches have outstanding maintenance items and what is the highest risk?
prompts.9: What operational problems followed the mobile ordering rollout?
prompts.10: The oat milk modifier bug lasted two weeks — what was the customer impact?
prompts.11: How did the mobile ordering rollout timing compound other operational issues?
prompts.12: What should be done differently before rolling out mobile ordering to Midlands?
prompts.13: Which branches had staffing shortages in Q1 2024?
prompts.14: How did understaffing compound the equipment failure at Leeds Central?
prompts.15: What are the staffing risks going into Easter trading?
prompts.16: Where do staffing issues and customer complaints overlap?
prompts.17: How did the espresso machine fault lead to drink quality complaints?
prompts.18: Which customer complaints were caused by supplier issues versus branch operational failures?
prompts.19: What is the most significant systemic risk facing the North England region?
prompts.20: Trace the full chain from the Wakefield depot failure to the Google Reviews impact
